questions.q: In which Indian state are the Ajanta Caves located?
questions.explanation: The Ajanta Caves are situated in the Aurangabad district of Maharashtra.

questions.q: The Ajanta Caves are associated with which religion?
questions.explanation: Ajanta is a complex of rock-cut Buddhist cave monuments.

questions.q: What is Ajanta most celebrated for?
questions.explanation: Ajanta is world-famous for its ancient wall paintings depicting the life of the Buddha.

questions.q: How were the Ajanta Caves created?
options: Built up with brick and mortar
questions.explanation: The caves were excavated and carved directly into a cliff of volcanic basalt rock.

questions.q: What international status do the Ajanta Caves hold?
questions.explanation: Ajanta was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1983.

questions.q: Many of the Ajanta paintings illustrate which stories?
options: The Jataka tales of the Buddha's past lives
questions.explanation: A large number of Ajanta's murals depict the Jataka tales, stories of the Buddha's previous births.

questions.q: The Ajanta Caves are carved into a cliff overlooking which kind of natural feature?
questions.explanation: The caves line a horseshoe-shaped cliff above the Waghora (Waghur) River gorge.

questions.q: Which river flows below the Ajanta Caves?
questions.explanation: The caves overlook the Waghora River, also spelled Waghur.

questions.q: Roughly how many rock-cut caves make up the Ajanta complex?
questions.explanation: The Ajanta complex consists of about 30 rock-cut caves, numbered 1 to 29 with later additions.

questions.q: What kind of imagery dominates Ajanta's sculptures and paintings?
options: Images of the Buddha and Buddhist themes
questions.explanation: The art at Ajanta centres on the Buddha and Buddhist religious themes.

questions.q: The earliest phase of excavation at Ajanta is usually dated to around which period?
questions.explanation: The first phase of caves dates to roughly the 2nd century BCE, in the Satavahana era.

questions.q: The two distinct building phases at Ajanta correspond to which two traditions of Buddhism?
questions.explanation: The earlier caves belong to the Hinayana phase and the later ones to the Mahayana phase.

questions.q: A British officer is credited with rediscovering the long-forgotten caves in 1819. What was his name?
questions.explanation: British officer John Smith stumbled upon the caves in 1819 while on a hunting expedition.

questions.q: What event led John Smith to the caves in 1819?
questions.explanation: Smith spotted the entrance to a cave while on a tiger hunt in the gorge.

questions.q: The two main types of caves at Ajanta are prayer halls and monasteries. What is the prayer hall called?
questions.explanation: A chaitya is a prayer or worship hall, while a vihara is a monastery.

questions.q: At Ajanta, what is a 'vihara'?
options: A worship hall with a stupa
A monastery or residential hall for monks
questions.explanation: A vihara is a monastic hall where monks lived, typically with cells around a central court.

questions.q: The chaitya (prayer) halls at Ajanta typically contain what at their far end?
questions.explanation: The chaitya halls house a stupa as the focus of worship at the rear of the hall.

questions.q: Cave 1 at Ajanta is renowned for a famous painting of which figure?
questions.explanation: Cave 1 contains the celebrated mural of the Bodhisattva Padmapani holding a lotus.

questions.q: Into what kind of rock were the Ajanta Caves cut?
questions.explanation: The caves were carved into the volcanic basalt rock of the Deccan Traps.

questions.q: The later, second phase of caves at Ajanta is generally attributed to the patronage of which dynasty?
questions.explanation: The Mahayana-phase caves were largely created under the patronage of the Vakataka dynasty.

questions.q: Which technique best describes how Ajanta's wall paintings were made?
options: Mineral pigments on a prepared plaster surface
questions.explanation: Artists applied natural mineral pigments onto a specially prepared plaster ground on the cave walls.

questions.q: Ajanta lies relatively close to which other famous group of rock-cut caves in Maharashtra?
questions.explanation: The Ellora Caves are another major rock-cut UNESCO site in the same region of Maharashtra.

questions.q: The Mahayana-phase patronage at Ajanta is especially linked to which Vakataka ruler?
questions.explanation: Much of the second-phase activity is associated with the reign of the Vakataka king Harishena in the late 5th century CE.

questions.q: The early, first-phase caves at Ajanta were patronised during the rule of which dynasty?
questions.explanation: The Hinayana-phase caves date to the period of Satavahana rule in the Deccan.

questions.q: Which two numbered caves are the oldest chaitya (prayer) halls of the early phase at Ajanta?
questions.explanation: Caves 9 and 10 are among the earliest chaitya halls, belonging to the first Hinayana phase.

questions.q: Cave 26 at Ajanta is noted for a large sculptural depiction of which scene?
options: The marriage of Shiva and Parvati
questions.explanation: Cave 26 contains a famous large carving of the reclining Buddha at his Parinirvana (death).

questions.q: Murals in Cave 17 famously include a scene of the Buddha receiving alms from whom?
options: His wife Yashodhara and son Rahula
questions.explanation: Cave 17 depicts the Buddha's return to Kapilavastu, met by his wife Yashodhara and son Rahula.

questions.q: Alongside Padmapani, Cave 1 features a famous painting of which other bodhisattva?
questions.explanation: Cave 1 pairs the Padmapani mural with an equally celebrated image of the Bodhisattva Vajrapani.

questions.q: Early scholarly copying of the Ajanta paintings is associated with which artist whose copies were later largely destroyed by fire?
questions.explanation: Major Robert Gill made painstaking copies of the murals, many of which were lost in a fire at the Crystal Palace.

questions.q: The second phase of activity at Ajanta is thought to have ended fairly abruptly, leaving several caves in what state?
questions.explanation: Work seems to have stopped suddenly in the late 5th century, leaving a number of caves unfinished.
